# Recent Developments 2024–2026 Affecting the Analysis

## Summary

Several developments since 2024 materially affect any assessment of Volt as a party fit for the user.

**Electoral trajectory**: Volt has now declined in three consecutive Tweede Kamer elections (3 seats → 2 → 1) while simultaneously growing its European Parliament presence (0 → 2 MEPs in 2024) and its membership base (~19,000 by early 2026). This bifurcation — strong European-level result, weak national result — is the defining structural fact about Volt in this period.

**Government context**: The Netherlands is now governed by the Jetten cabinet (D66/VVD/CDA minority, sworn in February 2026), replacing the Schoof cabinet (PVV/VVD/NSC/BBB) which collapsed in June 2025. Volt is in opposition. The new government's programme (*Aan de slag*) focuses on housing, nitrogen, economic policy, international security, and migration. The government is a minority cabinet dependent on case-by-case support from smaller parties. Volt's ability to influence policy from one opposition seat is limited but not zero — Dassen's lobby register motion achieved a parliamentary majority in March 2025, demonstrating that a single Volt MP can still build cross-party coalitions.

**Platform updates**: Volt issued a new election programme for the October 2025 elections. The main substantive update relative to 2023 is a significantly more urgent and detailed defense chapter, driven explicitly by US disengagement signals under Trump. The EU Army proposal moved from an aspiration to a concrete operational plan (60,000-person Rapid Deployment Capacity, Brussels HQ, shared intelligence). On Israel-Palestine, Volt's position evolved during 2025 — the party shifted from opposing a permanent arms embargo (June 2025) to supporting one (August 2025) before the election.

**European Parliament seats**: Volt's 2024 EP result (5.13%, 2 seats) is its best national result anywhere. The MEPs sit in the Renew Europe group. This makes the EP the party's primary institutional vehicle for legislative influence.

**2026 municipal elections** (March 18, 2026): Volt retained 2 Amsterdam city council seats and won its first Den Haag seat. National aggregate: ~2.9% in participating municipalities. The coalition in Arnhem (Volt, GroenLinks, D66, PvdD, PvdA, Arnhem Centraal) remains the only municipal governing coalition involving Volt.

**Current polling (April 2026)**: Volt polls at approximately 1.4% nationally, trending slightly upward (+0.3pp from the October 2025 result). Projection is ~2 seats. The party is not a significant factor in current coalition arithmetic.

**GL-PvdA merger**: GroenLinks-PvdA approved a full merger into "Progressief Nederland" (formal completion June 2026). This removes one major comparator party from the landscape and concentrates left-progressive representation. The new entity's programme is not yet available.

**Netherlands defense context**: The Netherlands has dramatically increased defense activity (F-16 deliveries to Ukraine, NATO Summit in The Hague committing to 5% GDP defense spending by 2035, CV90 procurement under European pooled structure). This makes Volt's long-standing European Army and 2% NATO norm positions look more mainstream than they did in 2021.

## Evidence

- [2025 Dutch general election — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_Dutch_general_election) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Schoof cabinet collapse timeline; snap election October 29, 2025; D66 and PVV tied at 26 seats; Volt 1 seat (1.10%).
- [2025–2026 Dutch cabinet formation — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025%E2%80%932026_Dutch_cabinet_formation) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Jetten cabinet: D66/VVD/CDA minority; sworn in February 23, 2026; coalition agreement *Aan de slag*; five-point agenda; minority status dependent on case-by-case support.
- [Netherlands: Political Developments and Data in 2024 — Wiley/EJPR](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2047-8852.70003) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Annual political data yearbook for 2024; covers election results and political development data through 2024.
- [Volt Netherlands — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volt_Netherlands) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Electoral results timeline; 2024 EP result (5.13%, 2 seats); membership growth to 19,004 (January 2026).
- [PolitPro — Netherlands polling, April 2026](https://politpro.eu/en/netherlands) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Current polling: Volt 1.4% aggregate; Ipsos March 9, 2026: 1.1%; Verian February 23, 2026: 1.3%; trend +0.3pp vs. election result; projected 2 seats.
- [Majority demands lobby register — NL Times, March 2025](https://nltimes.nl/2025/03/15/majority-tweede-kamer-demands-lobby-register-minister-resists) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Dassen's lobby register motion passed with majority support despite Volt's single seat.
- [LSE EuroPP — 2025 Dutch election analysis](https://blogs.lse.ac.uk/europpblog/2025/11/05/2025-dutch-election-jetten-wilders-not-quite-a-return-to-the-centre-netherlands/) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Far-right bloc maintained electoral size despite PVV losses; D66 framed as centrist winner; Volt not mentioned. Structural shift toward "technocratic liberal centrism vs. illiberal far-right" as dominant cleavage.
- [Amsterdam election March 2026 — DutchReview](https://dutchreview.com/news/amsterdam-2026-municipal-election-results/) (accessed 2026-04-19) — GroenLinks won Amsterdam; Volt retained 2 seats.
- [Local election results — DutchNews.nl, March 2026](https://www.dutchnews.nl/2026/03/the-results-in-the-big-five-cities-show-stability-on-the-left/) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Big-five city results; stability on the left.
- [NL Times — Amsterdam municipal elections 2026](https://nltimes.nl/2026/03/19/groenlinks-easily-wins-2026-amsterdam-city-council-election-coalition-gained-one-seat) (accessed 2026-04-19) — GL-PvdA largest Amsterdam party; coalition gained one seat.
- [Palestina en Israël: wat vindt Volt? — The Rights Forum](https://rightsforum.org/palestina-en-israel-wat-vindt-volt/) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Volt's position evolution on Israel/Gaza 2025: June 2025 voted against permanent embargo; August 2025 voted for it; genocide recognition from April 2025.
- [Een Europese krijgsmacht — Volt Nederland](https://voltnederland.org/standpunten/ga-voor-europa-in-een-veilige-wereld/een-europese-krijgsmacht) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Updated defense chapter emphasising US unreliability; Coalition of the Willing; European Army operational proposals.
- [Netherlands defense buildup — National Defense Magazine, 2025](https://www.nationaldefensemagazine.org/articles/2025/8/4/netherlands-focused-on-growing-defense-as-global-tensions-intensify) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Netherlands doubling defense personnel target; NATO summit 5% GDP commitment.
- [GroenLinks–PvdA — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GroenLinks%E2%80%93PvdA) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Merger referendum approved June 2025; formal merger into Progressief Nederland planned June 13, 2026.
- [Reactie coalitieakkoord D66-CDA-VVD 2026 — Eurodefense.nl](https://news.eurodefense.nl/2026/02/reactie-coalitieakkoord-d66-cda-vvd-dd.html) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Defense-sector reaction to the Jetten coalition agreement; confirms defense spending increase commitments.

## Uncertainty / conflicting sources

- **Reasons for Volt's Tweede Kamer decline**: No published analysis explains the vote share decline from 2.42% (2021) to 1.10% (2025). Possible explanations include: voter consolidation toward D66 (which surged); Volt's small size limiting legislative impact; the Gündoğan affair reputational damage (2022); and general pro-European voter preference for larger vehicles. None of these is confirmed by a dedicated study in available sources.
- **Jetten cabinet coalition agreement detail**: The full text of the *Aan de slag* agreement was not retrieved. It is unclear which of D66, VVD, or CDA's 2025 programme positions are reflected in the governing agreement, and which were traded away. The government's actual housing, climate, and defense policies may differ from the programme positions compared in this research package.
- **Volt's EP influence**: The two Volt MEPs (Renew Europe group) may exercise legislative influence in the European Parliament that is not captured in this Tweede Kamer-focused analysis. Their activity in the EP is not documented in detail in available sources.
- **Post-merger Progressief Nederland**: GL-PvdA's formal transition to Progressief Nederland (June 2026) is imminent. The council should treat GL-PvdA comparisons as reflecting a party that is transitioning and whose future policy positions are uncertain.
- **Current coalition dynamics**: As a minority government dependent on case-by-case support, the Jetten cabinet's legislative agenda is inherently unstable. Policy outcomes may diverge significantly from the programme commitments cited here.
